The town is also known for its prehistoric cave, known as the cave of Tito Bustillo, which is open to visitors all year round. Ribadesella is also visited for its history, sport activities, scenery, natural amenities, and food.

Ginataang ampalaya, is a Filipino vegetable stew made from bitter melon and tinapa (smoked fish) in coconut milk, bagoong alamang (shrimp paste), and spices. The dish can also be made with pork or shrimp and other vegetables. The dish is characteristically savory and slightly bitter due to the ingredients used. It is a type of ginataan.

Christian College is a K-12 independent school located over six campuses in Geelong, Victoria, Australia.

Three campuses are located in suburban Geelong: the Senior Campus at Waurn Ponds (Year 10–12), the Middle School in Highton (Year 5–9), and the Junior School in Belmont (Prep – Year 4). The Bellarine Campus is located in Drysdale (Prep- Year 9), and the Surf Coast campus is located in the coastal town of Torquay, which opened in 2018. There are also two other specialist campuses: "Back Creek" in Scotsburn (near Ballarat) is a dairy farm attended by Year 9 students, including Back Creek Cafe located in Meredith. The specialised Arts centre, Villa Paloma, is located off campus in Waurn Ponds a short distance from the Senior School, where VCE Arts students have their classes. Christian College also offers preschool kindergarten programs at the Junior School campus in Belmont (Williams House Kindergarten), and at the Surf Coast campus in Jan Juc (Butterfield House Kindergarten).

Christian College students are from a number of different areas in Geelong, these areas include Torquay, Drysdale, Anglesea, Ocean Grove, Queenscliff and Winchelsea as well as a majority from Geelong itself. Notable people to have gone to Christian College Geelong include AFL players Gary Ablett, Jr., Nathan Ablett, Matt Maguire, Taylor Adams, Connor Idun, AFLW player Renee Garing, netballer Tegan Philip (nee Caldwell), and Olympian Jakara Anthony.

*Sex, Money and Hip-Hop* is the 2014 studio album by rapper KXNG CROOKED (formerly Crooked I). Released under Treacherous C.O.B. and SMH Records, it features collaborations with Slaughterhouse and includes the socially conscious single *"I Can't Breathe,"* addressing police brutality. The album blends rap with political commentary.

Han Lao Da (born 22 October 1947), also known as Tan Tian, is a Singaporean playwright and *xiangsheng* (comic dialogue) artist. A student of Ma Ji, he has written numerous *xiangsheng* scripts and plays, promoting the art form in Singapore. He is also the principal of Han Language Centre.

Democratic centralism is a practice in which political decisions reached by voting processes are binding upon all members of the political party. It is mainly associated with Leninism, wherein the party's political vanguard of professional revolutionaries practised democratic centralism to elect leaders and officers, determine policy through free discussion, and decisively realise it through united action. Democratic centralism has also been practised by social democratic and democratic socialist parties as well. Scholars have disputed whether democratic centralism was implemented in practice in the Soviet Union and China, pointing to violent power struggles, backhanded political maneuvering, historical antagonisms and the politics of personal prestige in those regimes.

Sanan Suleymanov (Azerbaijani: *Sənan Süleymanov*) is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. A multiple medalist at the European Championships, he won bronze in 2021 and 2022, as well as silver at the 2021 Islamic Solidarity Games and the 2019 World U23 Championships. He competes in the 77 kg weight class.

"Heartache Tonight" is a song written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger and J. D. Souther, recorded by the Eagles and features Glenn Frey on lead vocals. The track was included on their album The Long Run and released as a single in 1979. It reached No. 1 on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 in November of that year and was certified Platinum by the Recording Industry Association of America representing one million copies sold. It was the Eagles' final chart-topping song on the Hot 100.

Ribadesella is a municipality in Asturias, Spain, located along the coast at the mouth of the River Sella. Known for its prehistoric Tito Bustillo Cave, a UNESCO World Heritage Site with Paleolithic cave paintings, the town has a rich history dating back to ancient times. The area was once inhabited by the Salaeni, an Asturian tribe mentioned by the Greek geographer Strabo.  

Today, Ribadesella is divided into distinct areas, including the historic Casco Antiguo (Old Town), the commercial center, and the Paseo Marítimo del Puerto (Port Promenade). The town hosts the famous International Descent of the Sella, a canoe race held every August, along with traditional festivals featuring Asturian music, dance, and cuisine. Other notable events include the Feria del Queso (Cheese Festival) and Semana Santa (Holy Week) celebrations. With a population of around 5,730, Ribadesella remains a cultural and historical hub in northern Spain.

"Everything Starts with an 'E'" is a song by E-Zee Possee featuring vocals from MC Kinky. It is considered by many to be the anthem of the acid house movement of the late 1980s, with the "E" in the title widely understood to refer to the drug Ecstasy (MDMA).

The song began life as an instrumental interpretation of Jeremy Healy and Simon Rogers' idea of house music after a trip to Ibiza which upon being presented to Boy George had vocals added to it by MC Kinky, Boy George and Eve Gallagher. This vocal version charted #15 on the UK Singles Chart, and although a music video was created, it was rarely broadcast due to its controversial lyrical content.

Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and professor at Stanford University, best known for his contributions to intelligence testing. He adapted the Binet-Simon Scale into the Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, popularizing the intelligence quotient (IQ). Terman conducted the Genetic Studies of Genius, a longitudinal study of gifted children, challenging stereotypes about intelligence and success. He also played a key role in World War I military testing and supported eugenics, advocating for intelligence-based social policies. His work influenced educational psychology, though his views on race and heredity remain controversial. His son, Frederick Terman, became a prominent engineer and academic.

Matt Seers (born 28 June 1974) is an Australian former professional rugby league footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played for the North Sydney Bears, Wests Tigers and Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, as a fullback, centre or five-eighth.
The Council on Religion and the Homosexual was a San Francisco-based organization founded in 1964 for the purpose of joining homosexual activists and religious leaders.

The former Reading Municipal Building is a historic building at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1885, this two-story brick building was the town's first municipal structure, housing the town offices, jail, and fire station. In 1918 all functions except fire services moved out of the building. It now serves as Reading's Pleasant Street Senior Center. The building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.

The Amos Baldwin House is a historic 18th-century Colonial home in Norfolk, Connecticut. Built around 1765, it features a gambrel roof, central chimney, and well-preserved interior details. Once owned by farmer and politician Amos Baldwin, it later housed a private boys' school in the 1940s. The house was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 2016.

The Underground Railroad is a 2016 novel by American author Colson Whitehead. It follows Cora, a young enslaved woman who escapes a Georgia plantation via the Underground Railroad—depicted as a literal subterranean railway. Pursued by a relentless slave catcher, she travels through different states, each representing a distinct historical and allegorical vision of racial oppression in America. The novel blends realism with speculative fiction, exploring themes of slavery, freedom, and resilience.  

Widely acclaimed, it won the 2017 Pulitzer Prize for Fiction and the 2016 National Book Award for Fiction, among other honors. Critics praised its powerful commentary on America’s past and present. In 2021, it was adapted into a limited series by Barry Jenkins for Amazon Prime Video.

Ebenezer Norman is a Liberian philanthropist, humanitarian, public speaker, and founder of the education non-profit A New Dimension of Hope (NDHope). He is involved in humanitarian efforts in Liberia and throughout West Africa, notably for efforts to build schools in attenuated or war-torn communities after the Second Liberian Civil War in 2003 and the Ebola virus epidemic in Liberia in 2015.

Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and author. He was noted as a pioneer in educational psychology in the early 20th century at the Stanford Graduate School of Education. He is best known for his revision of the Stanford–Binet Intelligence Scales and for initiating the longitudinal study of children with high IQs called the Genetic Studies of Genius. He was a prominent eugenicist and was a member of the Human Betterment Foundation. He also served as president of the American Psychological Association. A Review of General Psychology survey, published in 2002, ranked Terman as the 72nd most cited psychologist of the 20th century, in a tie with G. Stanley Hall.

Supersingular isogeny Diffie–Hellman key exchange (SIDH or SIKE) is an insecure proposal for a post-quantum cryptographic algorithm to establish a secret key between two parties over an untrusted communications channel. It is analogous to the Diffie–Hellman key exchange, but is based on walks in a supersingular isogeny graph and was designed to resist cryptanalytic attack by an adversary in possession of a quantum computer. Before it was broken, SIDH boasted one of the smallest key sizes of all post-quantum key exchanges; with compression, SIDH used 2688-bit public keys at a 128-bit quantum security level. SIDH also distinguishes itself from similar systems such as NTRU and Ring-LWE by supporting perfect forward secrecy, a property that prevents compromised long-term keys from compromising the confidentiality of old communication sessions. These properties seemed to make SIDH a natural candidate to replace Diffie–Hellman (DHE) and elliptic curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDHE), which are widely used in Internet communication. However, SIDH is vulnerable to a devastating key-recovery attack published in July 2022 and is therefore insecure.

Haemulinae is a subfamily of the Haemulidae and consists of the genera of that family which are regarded as being of New World origin, although they are now widespread. The subfamily is distinguished from the Plectorhynchinae by having a short dorsal fin which contains 13-16 soft rays, as opposed to the long dorsal fin with 17-26 soft rays of the subfamily Plectorhynchinae.

Howard John Ehmke (April 24, 1894 – March 17, 1959) was an American baseball pitcher. He played professional baseball for 16 years from 1914 to 1930, including 15 seasons in Major League Baseball for the Buffalo Blues (1915), Detroit Tigers (1916–1917, 1919–1922), Boston Red Sox (1923–1926), and Philadelphia Athletics (1926–1930).

Ehmke compiled a career win–loss record of 166–166 with a 3.75 earned run average (ERA). His greatest success was with the Red Sox, including a no-hitter and his only 20-win season in 1923. Ehmke still holds the American League record for fewest hits allowed (one) in two consecutive starts. Ehmke also ranks sixteenth all-time in hitting batters. He hit 137 batters in his career and led the American League in the category seven times, including a career-high 23 in 1922. Ehmke is best known for being the surprise starter who won Game 1 of the 1929 World Series for the Athletics at the age of 35.

After retiring from baseball, he started his own company that began making tarpaulins to cover baseball diamonds during rain.

CALSTAR is a nonprofit air medical transport service based in McClellan, California. Founded in 1983, it operates helicopters and fixed-wing aircraft across Northern California, Central California, and Northern Nevada. Acquired by REACH in 2016, it is now part of Global Medical Response.

On 23 May 2020, the dead body of 13-year-old girl Angira Pasi was found hanging from a tree in Devdaha, Nepal. The day before that, she was reportedly raped by 25-year-old Birendra Bhar, after which the local residents and ward member Amar Bahadur Chaudhary held a discussion and decided that Pasi was to be married to Bhar. After her death, the police initially refused to register the case; police only registered it after public outrage.

Theodorick Bland (December 2, 1708 – 1784), also known as Theodorick Bland, Sr. or Theodorick Bland of Cawsons, was Virginia planter who served as a member of the first Virginia Senate, as well as a militia officer and clerk of Prince George County, Virginia.

Alnus glutinosa, the common alder, black alder, European alder, European black alder, or just alder, is a species of tree in the family Betulaceae, native to most of Europe, southwest Asia and northern Africa. It thrives in wet locations where its association with the bacterium Frankia alni enables it to grow in poor quality soils. It is a medium-sized, short-lived tree growing to a height of up to 30 metres (98 feet). It has short-stalked rounded leaves and separate male and female flowers in the form of catkins. The small, rounded fruits are cone-like and the seeds are dispersed by wind and water.

The common alder provides food and shelter for wildlife, with a number of insects, lichens and fungi being completely dependent on the tree. It is a pioneer species, colonising vacant land and forming mixed forests as other trees appear in its wake. Eventually common alder dies out of woodlands because the seedlings need more light than is available on the forest floor. Its more usual habitat is forest edges, swamps and riverside corridors. The timber has been used in underwater foundations and for manufacture of paper and fibreboard, for smoking foods, for joinery, turnery and carving. Products of the tree have been used in ethnobotany, providing folk remedies for various ailments, and research has shown that extracts of the seeds are active against pathogenic bacteria.

The Divine Wings of Tragedy is the third studio album by progressive metal band Symphony X, released in November 1996 through Zero Corporation (Japan) and March 1997 through Inside Out Music (Europe). A remastered edition was reissued on September 13, 2004, through Inside Out, and again in September 17, 2012, as part of a double-LP vinyl release.
Animal Control Products Limited is a state-owned enterprise of the New Zealand government. Its main business is the manufacture and sale of pest management products.

Danielson Provincial Park is a provincial park in Saskatchewan, Canada, established in 1971. Located near the town of Outlook, the park offers a variety of recreational activities, including camping, swimming, boating, and hiking. It features three main campgrounds—Shady Lane, Bayside, and Elmview—with amenities such as electric hookups, potable water, showers, and sani-dumps. The park also includes a sandy beach, picnic area, boat launch, and fish cleaning station. Two main trails run through the park: the Great Trail, part of the Trans Canada Trail, and the Prairie View Nature Trail. Managed by Saskatchewan Parks, it serves as a popular destination for outdoor enthusiasts.

"Romeo & Juliet" is a song recorded by Kosovo-Albanian rapper Mozzik and Swiss-Albanian rapper Loredana. An Albanian and German rap song, it was solely written by the rappers themselves. German producers Miksu and Macloud were additionally hired for the song's composition and production process.

Latastia is a genus of lizards of the family Lacertidae. Species of this genus are distributed in Africa (Egypt, Ethiopia, Djibouti, Eritrea, Guinea-Bissau, Cameroon, Kenya, Malawi, Mali, Mozambique, Niger, Zambia, Senegal, Zimbabwe, Somalia, Sudan, South Sudan, Tanzania) but one subspecies (Latastia longicaudata andersonii) lives in Yemen. Collectively, they are known as long-tailed lizards.

Solder paste is a mixture of powdered solder suspended in flux, used in the assembly of printed circuit boards (PCBs). It is applied through stencil printing, where precise deposits are made before components are placed and reflow soldered. The paste's composition, particle size, and flux type (rosin, water-soluble, or no-clean) affect print quality and joint reliability. Common defects include bridging, insufficient solder, and head-in-pillow failures. Solder paste must be stored refrigerated to prevent oxidation and degradation. Inspection systems, such as solder paste inspection (SPI), help minimize defects by verifying deposit volumes before reflow. Lead-free alloys are now standard due to environmental regulations.

"Audi" is a song by American rapper Smokepurpp, released on May 17, 2017, as a single from his mixtape *Deadstar*. Produced by Ronny J, the track received positive reviews and was later certified Gold by the RIAA. A sequel, *"Audi II"*, was released in 2019.
Nabaganga (Lang-bn) is the fourth-biggest river in Bangladesh and a tributary of Mathabhanga.

Mount Hope is a granite outcrop located to the north of Pyramid Hill in northern Victoria, Australia. The outcrop lies within the 126 hectare Mount Hope Nature Conservation Reserve which is managed by Parks Victoria.

The reserve has limited facilities, including picnic tables and an unsealed parking area at the foot of the outcrop which is accessed from the unsealed Old Leitchville Road. Activities include rock climbing and bird observing.

Communications facilities for Ambulance Victoria, Goulburn Murray Water and the Department of Sustainability and Environment are located near the mountain summit.

Bakhtyuras Besikbayev (1920 – 26 June 1941) was a Kazakh Soviet Air Force gunner and radio operator during World War II. Serving in the 207th Long-Range Bomber Aviation Regiment, he was killed in action during the Battle of Białystok–Minsk. Initially missing in action, his crew was later identified as having possibly performed the first *fire taran* (suicidal ramming attack) against German forces, a feat initially attributed to Nikolai Gastello. Posthumously, Besikbayev was awarded the title Hero of Kazakhstan (1998) and Hero of the Russian Federation (1996).
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the National Football League.

"Stay" is a song written by Bob Khozouri and Mark Stevens, originally recorded by American singer Glenn Jones and released in 1990. It reached number six on the US Billboard Hot Black Singles chart. The song became a worldwide hit for British girl group Eternal in 1993 and 1994.

"Let It Go" is a 2010 jazz-influenced Cantopop song by Hong Kong singer Jacky Cheung, released as the fourth single from his album *Private Corner*. Co-written by Roxanne Seeman, Daniel Lindstrom, and Daniele Musto, with Cantonese lyrics by Kenny So, the song blends English and Cantonese lyrics. It peaked at number 16 on Hong Kong's HMV sales chart and was performed on Cheung's *Private Corner Mini Concert* and *1/2 Century Tour*.

Meckenbach is an Ortsgemeinde – a municipality belonging to a Verbandsgemeinde, a kind of collective municipality – in the Bad Kreuznach district in Rhineland-Palatinate, Germany. It belongs to the Verbandsgemeinde Kirner Land, whose seat is in the town of Kirn.

The New South Wales State Heritage Register, also known as NSW State Heritage Register, is a heritage list of places in the state of New South Wales, Australia, that are protected by New South Wales legislation, generally covered by the Heritage Act 1977 and its 2010 amendments. The register is administered by the Heritage Council of NSW via Heritage NSW, a division of the Government of New South Wales Department of Planning and Environment.

The register was created in 1999 and includes items protected by heritage schedules that relate to the State, and to regional and to local environmental plans. As a result, the register contains over 20,000 statutory-listed items in either public or private ownership of historical, cultural, and architectural value. Of those items listed, approximately 1,785 items are listed as significant items for the whole of New South Wales; with the remaining items of local or regional heritage value. The items include buildings, objects, monuments, Aboriginal places, gardens, bridges, landscapes, archaeological sites, shipwrecks, relics, bridges, streets, industrial structures and conservation precincts.

Typically, an item will first attract local listing, then regional or State listing. If the item is of significance to the nation, the State will advocate for listing on the Australian National Heritage List or the Commonwealth Heritage List. Finally, if the item is of global significance, the Australian Government will advocate for the item to be listed on the UNESCO World Heritage List.

Maximum PC is an American monthly magazine focused on computing and technology, published by Future US since 1996. Originally launched as *boot* in August 1996, it was rebranded as *Maximum PC* in September 1998. Based in San Francisco, the magazine is known for its product reviews, which rate hardware and software on a scale of 1 to 10, with exceptional products receiving a "Kick Ass" award. The only product to ever receive an "11" was *Half-Life 2* in 2005. Reviews include a humorous "Pros and Cons" section featuring wordplay and pop culture references.  

With a claimed circulation of 250,000 in 2010, *Maximum PC* offers free PDF archives of past issues on its website. The magazine also had a short-lived Italian edition (2004–2005) and a quarterly spin-off, *Maximum Tech* (2010–2011). Notable contributors include Zak Storey (editor) and freelance writers such as Jarred Walton and Ian Evenden.

Drapers' Academy is a mixed secondary school and sixth form in Romford, London. Established in 2010, it is sponsored by the Drapers' Company and Queen Mary, University of London. The academy specializes in science and mathematics and is part of the Drapers' Multi-Academy Trust.

Stone Brothers Racing (SBR) was an Australian motor racing team that competed in the V8 Supercars Championship from 1998 to 2012. Founded by brothers Ross and Jim Stone after acquiring Alan Jones Racing, SBR became one of the most successful teams in the series, winning three drivers' championships (2003, 2004, 2005) and 23 race victories. Notable drivers included Marcos Ambrose, Russell Ingall, and James Courtney, with highlights including Bathurst 1000 wins in 1998 and 2004. The team also ran customer entries for Larkham Motor Sport and Team Kiwi Racing. After selling to Erebus Motorsport in 2012, Ross Stone remained involved in motorsport, while Jim Stone established Matt Stone Racing in the Development Series.
Cristián Caro Cordero (born 16 February 1943) is a Chilean prelate of the Catholic Church who was Archbishop of Puerto Montt from 2001 to June 2018.

Puzzle Mansion is a toy museum in Tagaytay, Philippines, housing over 1,500 jigsaw puzzles. Founded in 2012 by Georgina Gil-Lacuna, it once held the Guinness World Record for the largest puzzle collection. The museum also operates as a bed-and-breakfast.

Gorky Central Park of Culture and Leisure (Russian: Центральный парк культуры и отдыха имени Горького, romanized: Tsentralny park kultury i otdykha imeni Gorkogo, IPA: [tsɨnˈtraljnɨj ˈpark kʊljˈturɨ i ˈoddɨxə ˈimɪnɪ ˈɡorkova]) is a central urban park in Rostov-on-Don, Rostov oblast, Russia, named after Maxim Gorky. It is the eldest park in Rostov-on-Don. The park was established in 1813 on the skirts of the city. Now it is located in center of Rostov-on-Don between Pushkinskaya street (Gorky Park North), Bolshaya Sadovaya street (Gorky Park South), Budyonovskiy avenue (Gorky Park West) and Semashko lane (Gorky Park East). Since 1998 the park is protected as an object of cultural heritage of regional value.

Senegalese Americans are Americans of Senegalese descent, numbering 18,091 as of 2019. Many trace their ancestry to enslaved West Africans trafficked through Senegambia during the transatlantic slave trade. Modern immigration began in the 20th century, with communities forming in New York City (particularly Harlem's "Little Senegal"), Chicago, and New Jersey. Most Senegalese Americans are Muslim, speak Wolof or French, and work in trade, business, or professional fields. Organizations like the Association of Senegalese in America and Tékkil support community development and cultural preservation.
The Weatherly 201 is an agricultural aircraft developed in the 1960s, featuring a radial engine and a glassfibre chemical hopper for crop dusting.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American politician who served as the mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota, from 2000 to 2020. A Republican, he was the city's longest-serving mayor, overseeing major projects including the expansion of the Mall of America, the Blue Line light rail, and infrastructure improvements. Before becoming mayor, he served on Bloomington's city council and planning commission.

Dr Abdulai Osman Conteh (born 5 August 1945) is a lawyer and politician from Sierra Leone. He is a former vice president of Sierra Leone, who served under President Joseph Saidu Momoh before he was ousted by the military junta in 1992. Conteh later spent a considerable number of years in Belize, where he served as the country's chief justice before returning to Sierra Leone.

Danielson Provincial Park is located at the northern end of Lake Diefenbaker in the southern part of the Canadian province of Saskatchewan. The park surrounds the Gardiner Dam, which was built in 1967 and is among the largest embankment dams in Canada and the world. The South Saskatchewan River flows north beyond the park, towards Outlook and Saskatoon. Saskatchewan Highways 44, 45, and 219 provide access to the park. SaskPower's Coteau Creek Hydroelectric Station is located near the park.

The park opened to the public in 1971 and was named for former MLA Gustaf Herman Danielson. It is one if three provincial parks on Lake Diefenbaker, with the other two being Douglas Provincial Park and Saskatchewan Landing Provincial Park.

Danielson Provincial Park's Visitor Centre is located between Lake Diefenbaker and Coteau Bay, and features numerous interactive displays about the Gardiner Dam and the river systems of southern Saskatchewan in addition to a café and beach.
Kylie Nicole Sicangco Padilla (IPA-tl; born 25 January 1993) is a Filipino actress, singer and model. She is the daughter of actor Robin Padilla and Liezl Sicangco.

Farma season 12 was the twelfth season of the Slovak reality show *Farma*, airing on Markíza from August 31 to December 11, 2020. Hosted by Eva "Evelyn" Kramerová, it featured 20 contestants divided into City and Village teams, with a twist swapping their living conditions. Xénia Gregušová won the season.
The Neradje Mosque or Neradjes (Lang-el) is a historical Ottoman-era mosque located in the old town of Rethymno, Crete, Greece. It now serves as a music school.
Michael Spira is a British physician specializing in obesity management, general practice, and weight control, and serves as medical director of the Smart Clinics.

*The Show Must Go On* (2007) is a South Korean crime drama film directed by Han Jae-rim, starring Song Kang-ho as a gangster struggling to balance family life and criminal obligations. The film earned Song the Best Actor award at the 28th Blue Dragon Film Awards, where it also won Best Film. It grossed US$6.7 million and attracted over 1 million admissions in South Korea.

Solder paste is used in the manufacture of printed circuit boards to connect surface mount components to pads on the board. It is also possible to solder through-hole pin in paste components by printing solder paste in and over the holes. The sticky paste temporarily holds components in place; the board is then heated, melting the paste and forming a mechanical bond as well as an electrical connection. The paste is applied to the board by jet printing, stencil printing or syringe; then the components are put in place by a pick-and-place machine or by hand.

Dysbindin, encoded by the *DTNBP1* gene, is a protein involved in synaptic function and vesicle trafficking. It is associated with Hermansky–Pudlak syndrome type 7 and has been studied for its potential role in schizophrenia, though genetic links vary across populations. Dysbindin interacts with proteins such as SNAPAP, MUTED, and PLDN, and its dysfunction may affect dopamine receptor signaling and neural plasticity. Research suggests it plays a role in neuronal development and synaptic vesicle regulation, but its exact mechanisms remain under investigation.

The Chicago Ballers were a professional basketball team in the Junior Basketball Association (JBA), founded in 2018 by LaVar Ball. Based in Chicago, Illinois, they played one season under head coach Nicolas Colon before the league disbanded.
Maria José Aguiar (born 1948) is a Portuguese artist. Her work is marked by the use of feminist themes related to sex and gender issues.

Richard Stöhr (1874–1967) was an Austrian-American composer, music theorist, and educator. Born in Vienna to Jewish parents, he studied medicine before pursuing music under Robert Fuchs at the Vienna Academy of Music, where he later became a professor. A prolific composer, Stöhr wrote symphonies, chamber music, operas, and choral works, maintaining a late-Romantic style despite the rise of modernism. His *Harmonielehre* (1909) became a widely used music theory textbook.  

Forced out of Austria after the 1938 *Anschluss* due to his Jewish heritage, he emigrated to the U.S., teaching at the Curtis Institute of Music and Saint Michael’s College. Though many of his European works were published and performed, his later American compositions remained unpublished. His extensive diary and manuscripts are preserved in Austrian and U.S. archives. Posthumously, his music has seen renewed interest, with recordings and performances of his chamber works.
Machi (Chinese: 麻吉; pinyin: májí; Pe̍h-ōe-jī: Mâ-kiat) is a Taiwanese hip hop group that has nine active members, with two additional members on a hiatus from group activities.

Konanakunte, formerly a census town, is a suburb of Bangalore in the Indian state of Karnataka.The MP of this area is DK Suresh (brother of DK Shivakumar, former karnataka minister),mla for this area is M.Krishnappa, Corporator is Shashirekha Jayaram.
Kylie Nicole Sicangco Padilla (born January 25, 1993) is a Filipino actress, singer, and model known for her roles in *Encantadia* (2016), *Joaquin Bordado*, and *The Good Daughter*.

Domingos Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for La Liga club Getafe and the Portugal national team. A product of Sporting CP's academy, he previously played for Granada and had loan spells at Belenenses, Chaves, and Deportivo La Coruña.
"Motiv8" (pronounced "motivate") is a song by American rapper J. Cole, released on April 20, 2018 from his fifth studio album, KOD.

"Stay" is a song originally recorded by American singer Glenn Jones in 1990. It gained greater success in 1993 when British girl group Eternal released it as their debut single, peaking at number four on the UK Singles Chart and number 19 on the US *Billboard* Hot 100. The R&B-infused pop track received critical acclaim for its soulful vocals and catchy melody.

Patricia "Patt" Murphy Derian (née Murphy; August 12, 1929 – May 20, 2016) was an American civil rights and human rights activist who fought racism in Mississippi and went on to serve as Assistant Secretary of State for Human Rights and Humanitarian Affairs from 1977 to 1981. She was, remembered The Times of London, "a courageous champion of civil rights who took on some of the world's most brutal dictators in her role as a senior American diplomat."

The England Boxing National Amateur Championships (formerly the ABA Championships) is an annual boxing competition organized by England Boxing since 1881. Originally featuring four weight classes, it now includes eleven. Held at venues such as the Royal Albert Hall and Wembley Arena, it serves as a qualifier for the Four Nations Championship.

Frederick Twort (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist who discovered bacteriophages—viruses that infect bacteria—in 1915. Educated at St Thomas's Hospital, he later worked at the Brown Animal Sanatory Institution, researching bacterial mutation, growth factors, and Johne's disease. Despite his groundbreaking work on bacteriophages, his findings were initially overlooked until Félix d'Herelle's independent discovery. Twort also investigated viral cultivation and bacterial evolution. Elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1929, he faced declining support later in life and died in obscurity.

The King of Rome (Latin: *Rex Romae*) was the chief magistrate and ruler of the Roman Kingdom, holding supreme military, judicial, and religious authority. According to tradition, Rome was ruled by seven kings from its founding in 753 BC until the overthrow of Lucius Tarquinius Superbus in 509 BC, which led to the establishment of the Roman Republic. The king was elected by the Curiate Assembly, though the Senate played a key role in nomination. His powers included command of the army, administration of justice, and appointment of officials. After the monarchy's abolition, some religious functions were preserved in the office of *rex sacrorum*, while political authority shifted to consuls and other magistrates.

Dick Tracy vs. Crime, Inc. (1941) is a Republic Movie serial based on the Dick Tracy comic strip. It was directed by the team of William Witney and John English with Ralph Byrd reprising his role from the earlier serials. It was the last of the four Dick Tracy serials produced by Republic, although Ralph Byrd went on to portray the character again in two features and on television.

Ginataang ampalaya is a Filipino dish made from bitter melon cooked in coconut milk with smoked fish (tinapa), onions, garlic, and shrimp paste. Additional ingredients like pork, shrimp, or vegetables may be included. The bitter melon is often soaked to reduce its bitterness before cooking. It is a variation of ginataang gulay (vegetables in coconut milk) and is typically served with rice.

The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK public body (1995–2007) that managed large-scale scientific facilities, including the Diamond Light Source. It merged with PPARC in 2007 to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council (STFC).

The 2007 World Indoor Lacrosse Championship was the second World Indoor Lacrosse Championship, an international box lacrosse tournament organized by the Federation of International Lacrosse every four years. It took place between May 14 and 20 at the Halifax Metro Centre in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada. Canada was the defending champion and again beat the Iroquois Nationals in the final, this time 15–14 in overtime. Eight nations took part in this event, the six nations from the 2003 WILC and two newcomers - England and Ireland. There was an estimated 850,000 television viewers of the tournament in Canada and the games were streamed live in Europe.

In grammar, a supine is a form of verbal noun used in some languages. The term is most often used for Latin, where it is one of the four principal parts of a verb. The word refers to a position of lying on one's back (as opposed to'prone', lying face downward), but there exists no widely accepted etymology that explains why or how the term came to be used to also describe this form of a verb.

Hilal is an elevated station on the Fahrettin Altay—Evka 3 Line of the İzmir Metro in Konak. Located above the Turkish State Railways' Hilal junction, it is one of the ten original stations of the metro system. Connection to İZBAN commuter trains are available at Hilal railway station. Since Hilal is located in the middle of a railway junction, no direct connections to ESHOT city bus service is available. The closest bus stop is located on 1396th Street about 460 m (1,510 ft) northwest of the station.

Hilal station was opened on 22 May 2000.

Millstatt am See is a market town of the Spittal an der Drau District in Carinthia, Austria. The traditional health resort and spa town on Lake Millstatt is known for former Benedictine Millstatt Abbey, founded about 1070.
Wilhelm von Henke (1834–1896) was a German anatomist known for his work on the retropharyngeal space ("Henke's space") and his analysis of the *Venus de Milo* statue.

Navajo music is music made by Navajos, mostly hailing from the Four Corners region of the Southwestern United States and the territory of the Navajo Nation. While it traditionally takes the shape of ceremonial chants and echoes themes found in Diné Bahaneʼ, contemporary Navajo music includes a wide range of genres, ranging from country music to rock and rap, performed in both English and Navajo.

Terranova is a German electronic music duo formed in Berlin in 1996 by Fetisch and Shapemod. Known for their eclectic style, they gained recognition with early releases like *Tokyo Tower* (1997) and their *DJ-Kicks* mix album (1998). Their debut LP, *Close the Door* (1999), featured collaborations with artists such as Tricky, Stereo MCs, and Nicolette Krebitz. Subsequent albums, including *Hitchhiking Nonstop with no Particular Destination* (2002) and *Hotel Amour* (2012), showcased their evolving sound across labels like !K7, Ministry of Sound, and Kompakt. Terranova is also known for side projects like *The Lotterboys* and *The Lottergirls*.

Sapporo Television Broadcasting (札幌テレビ放送株式会社, *Sapporo Terebi Hōsō*), commonly known as STV, is a Japanese television network headquartered in Sapporo, Hokkaido. Founded on April 8, 1958, it began broadcasting on April 1, 1959, as a dual affiliate of Nippon News Network (NNN) and Fuji News Network (FNN). It later became a full NNN member in 1966 and launched radio operations in 1962. STV was the first broadcaster in Japan to use an automatic advertising system and has consistently ranked first in Hokkaido's TV ratings since 1993. The network expanded into digital broadcasting in 2006 and spun off its radio division in 2005. STV also played a role in covering the 1972 Sapporo Winter Olympics.
Allan B. Hubbard (born September 8, 1947) finished his term as the Assistant to President George W. Bush for Economic Policy and Director of the National Economic Council at the end of 2007.

The Aleutian tern (*Onychoprion aleuticus*) is a medium-sized seabird in the family Laridae, breeding along the Pacific coasts of Alaska and Siberia and wintering in Southeast Asia and Australia. Measuring 32–39 cm (13–15 in) in length with a wingspan of 75–80 cm (30–31 in), it has a black cap, gray underparts, and a distinctive dark bar on its secondaries. Known for its soft, whistled calls, it nests in loose colonies on coastal meadows and islands, often alongside Arctic terns.  

The species is highly migratory, with poorly understood wintering grounds, though sightings occur in Indonesia, Malaysia, and Hong Kong. Listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, its populations have declined sharply, possibly due to habitat disturbance, predation, and human activity. Conservation efforts include tracking studies to determine migration routes and breeding behaviors. Its diet consists mainly of small fish, crustaceans, and insects, caught by surface-dipping while in flight.

Gorky Park is a public park in Rostov-on-Don, Russia, established in 1813 on former wasteland. Originally consisting of separate gardens, it was later unified into Upper and Lower sections. The park features landscaped pathways, fountains, sculptures, and historic structures, including a 19th-century rotunda and an ornamental entrance designed by Nikolai Doroshenko. Spanning 12.2 hectares (30 acres), it includes attractions such as playgrounds, amusement rides, an observatory, and monuments, including one to Lenin. The park also hosts art exhibitions and cultural events.
Cipher runes, or cryptic runes, are the cryptographical replacement of the letters of the runic alphabet.

The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK government body that carried out civil research in science and engineering.

On 1 April 2007 CCLRC merged with PPARC to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council.

Pennsylvania Route 934 (PA 934) is a 10.873-mile (17.498 km) state highway in Lebanon County, Pennsylvania, maintained by the Pennsylvania Department of Transportation (PennDOT). The route begins at an intersection with U.S. Route 322 (US 322) and PA 241 in South Annville Township and heads north through the community of Annville, passing Lebanon Valley College and intersecting US 422. Continuing northwest, PA 934 crosses the Swatara Creek and meets US 22 before terminating at an interchange with Interstate 81 (I-81) near Fort Indiantown Gap.  

Originally designated in 1928, PA 934 initially ran from US 22 (now US 422) in Annville to Bellegrove. It was later extended south to US 322/PA 241 and north to I-81, replacing portions of PA 343. The route underwent realignments in the 1960s and 1970s before being shortened to its current northern terminus.

The architecture of Poland includes modern and historical monuments of architectural and historical importance.

Several important works of Western architecture, such as the Wawel Hill, the Książ and Malbork castles, cityscapes of Toruń, Zamość, and Kraków are located in the country. Some of them are UNESCO World Heritage Sites. Now Poland is developing modernist approaches in design with architects like Daniel Libeskind, Karol Żurawski, and Krzysztof Ingarden.
River City Labs is a Brisbane-based coworking space and startup hub founded in 2012 by Steve Baxter.
Stevie Anderson (born May 12, 1970) is a former American football wide receiver who played in the NFL for the New York Jets and Arizona Cardinals.

Mam Tor is a 517 m (1,696 ft) hill near Castleton in the High Peak of Derbyshire, England. Its name means "mother hill", so called because frequent landslips on its eastern face have resulted in a multitude of "mini-hills" beneath it. These landslips, which are caused by unstable lower layers of shale, also give the hill its alternative name of Shivering Mountain.

In 1979, the continual battle to maintain the A625 road (Sheffield to Chapel en le Frith) on the crumbling eastern side of the hill was lost when the road officially closed as a through-route, with the Fox House to Castleton section of the road being re-designated as the A6187.

The hill is crowned by a late Bronze Age and early Iron Age univallate hill fort, and two Bronze Age bowl barrows. At the base of the Tor and nearby are four show caves: Blue John Cavern, Speedwell Cavern, Peak Cavern and Treak Cliff Cavern where lead, Blue John, fluorspar and other minerals were once mined. Mam Tor was declared to be one of the Seven Wonders of the Peak by Thomas Hobbes in his 1636 book De Mirabilibus Pecci.

Simon Jenkins rates the panorama from Kinder Scout to Stanage Edge as one of the top ten in England.

*Aadmi* (1993) is a Hindi action drama film directed by Arshad Khan, starring Mithun Chakraborty as Vijay, an honest man framed for murder who seeks justice against corrupt officials. The film features music by Jatin–Lalit and includes a rare positive role by Shakti Kapoor.

Harloe (born Jessica Ashley Karpov; July 10, 1992) is an American singer, songwriter, and music producer known for her contemporary R&B and pop sound. After initially performing as Jessica Ashley, she rebranded as Harloe in 2016, releasing singles like *"All in My Feelings"* and *"One More Chance."* She has written for artists such as Robin Schulz and contributed to *League of Legends'* virtual group K/DA. Signed to Roc Nation, she released her debut EP, *Rivers Run Dry*, in 2020.
Mario Adrián Vázquez (born June 15, 1977) is an American singer from The Bronx, New York City. He competed on American Idol's fourth season, which aired on the FOX network in early 2005.
The Puzzle Mansion is a museum in Tagaytay, Cavite, Philippines which hosts a collection of jigsaw puzzle which from 2012 to 2017 was recognized to be the biggest in the world according to Guinness.

The E3 Tug Project is a joint R&D project between Smit International, Damen Shipyards Gorinchem and Alewijnse Marine Technology. The goal of the project is to create an energy efficient tugboat engine to be integrated into future tugboat designs. The purpose of which is to alleviate unnecessary ship pollution by studying the operational patterns of harbor tug use. The Port of Rotterdam is spearheading the project with a goal that by the 2020 it will have reduced its air pollution by ten percent.

The Highwomen is the debut studio album by country music supergroup the Highwomen, made up of band members Brandi Carlile, Natalie Hemby, Maren Morris, and Amanda Shires. It was released on September 6, 2019, by Elektra Records.

Nicholas Nichols Cox (January 6, 1837 – May 2, 1912) was an American attorney, Confederate officer, and Democratic politician who served five terms in the U.S. House of Representatives from Tennessee (1891–1901).

Sex, Money and Hip-Hop is the second studio album by rapper and Slaughterhouse member KXNG CROOKED (also known as Crooked I). The album was released on December 16, 2014 and it was executive produced by KXNG CROOKED himself along with Mike Smith and Jonathan Hay.

Ralfi Pagán (born Rafael Pagán; 1946–1978) was a Bronx, New York-based Latin soul and salsa singer of Puerto Rican and Cuban parentage who was active from the mid-1960s until his death in 1978. He specialized in soul ballads sung both in Spanish and English and released five albums during the 1970s. He is well known for the duet with Sylvia Robinson entitled Soul Je T'aime and for his solo reworking of Make It With You previously recorded by David Gates and Bread. Ralfi toured with legendary music producer and promoter Eddie Torres for several years throughout the Southwest United States. Prior to leaving on tour Ralfi visited Eddie’s home to share he was leaving to Colombia. Eddie told Ralfi he had a bad feeling about the trip. Eddie advised Ralfi not to go. Ralfi went to Colombia against Torres’ advise. In 1978, he was murdered while on a promotional tour in Colombia, South America. His murder remains unsolved.
Ruby is a color that is a representation of the color of the cut and polished ruby gemstone and is a shade of red or pink.

Petr Rada (born 21 August 1958) is a Czech football manager and former defender, currently in charge of Dukla Prague. As a player, he spent most of his career at Dukla Prague, winning two Czechoslovak First League titles (1979, 1982) and earning 11 caps for Czechoslovakia. After retiring, he became a manager, leading clubs such as Slavia Prague, Teplice, and Jablonec, where he reached the 2007 Czech Cup final. He also briefly managed the Czech Republic national team (2008–2009) but was dismissed after poor World Cup qualifying results. Rada has had multiple spells at various Czech clubs, including Sparta Prague and Slovan Liberec. His son, Filip Rada, is also a professional footballer.

The Reading Municipal Building is a historic town hall and fire station located at 49 Pleasant Street in Reading, Massachusetts. Built in 1883 in a Renaissance Revival and Stick/Eastlake style, it was designed by architect Horace G. Wadlin and originally housed town offices, a jail, and a fire station. After the town offices moved in 1918, it remained a fire station before being repurposed as a senior center. It was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1984.

The Amos Baldwin House is a historic house at 92 Goshen Street East in Norfolk, Connecticut, United States. Built about 1765, it is an important surviving example of colonial architecture in the community, and is one of its oldest buildings with a gambrel roof. It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2016.

The Burrumbeet Cup is a thoroughbred horse race, held under handicap conditions over a distance of 1800 metres at the Burrumbeet racecourse, Burrumbeet, Victoria, Australia on New Year's Day every year.

The 2020 Petrogas Regional Burrumbeet Cup will be held on 1 January 2020 and will be the 130th running of the race.

Prize money for the 2020 Burrumbeet Cup is $25,000 and Petrogas Regional is race sponsor. Petrogas Regional became the Burrumbeet Cup sponsor from 2015 onwards after the decision by Scott Petroleum to end their Burrumbeet Cup sponsorship following the running of the 2014 Burrumbeet Cup.

The Burrumbeet Park & Windermere Racing Club (BP&WRC) administer racing at Burrumbeet under the jurisdiction of Racing Victoria and all 14 committee members are volunteers. President of the BP & WRC is Rod McKinnon and club secretary is Paul Brumby.

Kuressaare (IPA-et) is a town on Saaremaa island in Estonia. It is the administrative centre of Saaremaa Parish and the capital of Saare County. Kuressaare is the westernmost town in Estonia. The recorded population on 1 January 2018 was 13,276.

The town is situated on the southern coast of Saaremaa island, facing the Gulf of Riga of the Baltic Sea, and is served by the Kuressaare Airport, Roomassaare harbour, and Kuressaare yacht harbour.

*Shade* (2003) is an American crime thriller film directed by Damian Nieman, starring Stuart Townsend, Gabriel Byrne, Thandiwe Newton, Jamie Foxx, and Sylvester Stallone. The plot follows a group of con artists attempting to swindle a high-stakes poker game, only to become entangled in deception and betrayal. Produced by RKO Pictures, the film received mixed reviews and grossed $458,144 worldwide. It was later released on Blu-ray in 2019.
ACE (Advanced Computer Entertainment) was a multi-format computer and video game magazine first published in the United Kingdom by Future Publishing and later acquired by EMAP.

Stowaway is a 1936 American musical drama film directed by William A. Seiter. The screenplay by William M. Conselman, Nat Perrin, and Arthur Sheekman is based on a story by Samuel G. Engel. The film is about a young orphan called "Ching Ching" (Shirley Temple) who meets wealthy playboy Tommy Randall (Robert Young) in Shanghai and then accidentally stows away on the ocean liner he is travelling on. The film was hugely successful, and is available on videocassette and DVD.

Navajo music encompasses traditional ceremonial songs, children's music, peyote songs, and contemporary genres. Traditional music is primarily vocal, accompanied by instruments like drums and rattles, used in sacred healing ceremonies led by *hataałii* (medicine people). Other forms include the Shoegame songs, children's chants, and peyote songs tied to the Native American Church. Modern Navajo musicians explore rock, hip-hop, metal, and other genres, blending tradition with contemporary styles.

The Underground Railroad is a historical fiction novel by American author Colson Whitehead, published by Doubleday in 2016. The alternate history novel tells the story of Cora, a slave in the Antebellum South during the 19th century, who makes a bid for freedom from her Georgia plantation by following the Underground Railroad, which the novel depicts as a rail transport system with safe houses and secret routes. The book was a critical and commercial success, hitting the bestseller lists and winning several literary awards, including the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction, the National Book Award for Fiction, the Arthur C. Clarke Award, and the 2017 Andrew Carnegie Medal for Excellence. A TV miniseries adaptation, written and directed by Barry Jenkins, was released in May 2021.

Paul Thomson (June 29, 1916 – May 31, 2008) was an American exotic fruit enthusiast, self-taught horticulturist and botanist, fruit farmer, and the co-founder of the California Rare Fruit Growers Association, a group of amateur horticulturists which now has more than 3,000 members in approximately 35 countries. Thomson is credited with helping to expand the farming of exotic fruits in California – everything from cherimoyas to longans to pitahayas. Thomson also wrote one of the few literary treatments of the genus Dudleya, and was an avid collector of the plants.

Giosuè Giuppone (Calco, 29 September 1878 – Wirwignes, 16 September 1910) was an Italian track cyclist, motorcycle racer and race car driver in the early 1900s. He was racing Peugeot motorcycle and automobiles until his death in 1910.

The England Boxing National Amateur Championships previously known as the ABA Championships is the premier boxing tournament hosted annually by England Boxing. The Championships are 'open' class: any boxer who is registered with a club registered with England Boxing (aged over 17 years by the 1 October) can enter.

Anything But Conservative (ABC) was a strategic voting campaign in Canada, most prominently led by Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Danny Williams during the 2008 federal election. The campaign urged voters to support any party other than the Conservative Party in response to Prime Minister Stephen Harper's broken promise on equalization payments to the province. The effort successfully denied the Conservatives any seats in Newfoundland and Labrador, though they still won a minority government nationally. Similar grassroots movements advocating strategic voting emerged in later elections, including the 2015 federal election, where anti-Conservative coordination contributed to the Liberal Party's majority victory. The ABC campaign highlighted tensions between provincial and federal Conservatives and influenced electoral tactics in Canadian politics.

Lyme is a town along the Connecticut River in Grafton County, New Hampshire, United States. The population was 1,745 as of the 2020 census. Lyme is home to the Chaffee Natural Conservation Area. The Dartmouth Skiway is in the eastern part of town, near the village of Lyme Center. The Appalachian Trail passes through the town's heavily wooded eastern end.

Chas Henry (born January 6, 1989) is a former American football punter who played in the NFL for the Philadelphia Eagles (2011–2012). A consensus All-American and Ray Guy Award winner at the University of Florida, he was part of the Gators' 2008 BCS national championship team. Henry also briefly played for the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in 2013.

The Aleutian tern (Onychoprion aleuticus) is a migratory bird living in the subarctic region of the globe most of the year. It is frequently associated with the Arctic tern, which it closely resembles. While both species have a black cap, the Aleutian tern may be distinguished by its white forehead (although juvenile Arctic terns also have white foreheads). During breeding season, the Arctic terns have bright red bills, feet, and legs while those of the Aleutian terns are black.

Onychoprion aleuticus has not been thoroughly studied yet. If its winter migratory range was completely unknown until the late 1980s; it is now known that many Aleutian terns spend the winter near the Equator in the western Pacific. The Aleutian tern breeds in wide-ranging coastal colonies only in Alaska and eastern Siberia. Although Alaskan and Siberian populations are not well monitored, both are thought to be in significant decline.

The National Trust for Scotland is a conservation charity founded in 1931 to protect Scotland's natural and cultural heritage. It owns and manages over 130 properties, including castles, gardens, and 180,000 acres of countryside, such as Glen Coe and St Kilda. With around 330,000 members, it operates similarly to the National Trust in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. The Trust relies on membership fees, donations, and volunteers to maintain its sites.

The Curse of Oak Island is a multi-season reality television series that chronicles an eclectic team of treasure hunters and their search for legendary treasure on Oak Island, off the shore of Nova Scotia, Canada. It is an American television production that premiered in Canada on the History network on January 6, 2014. The show features what is known as the Oak Island mystery, showing efforts to search for historical artifacts and treasure.

The tenth season premiered on November 15, 2022.

Gregg Henry (born May 6, 1952) is an American actor and musician known for his roles in television and film. With a career spanning over four decades, he has appeared in numerous TV series, including *The Riches*, *Scandal*, *The Killing*, and *White Collar*, as well as films such as *Guardians of the Galaxy*. He has also performed in theater, notably playing Julius Caesar in Shakespeare in the Park. Henry is married to theater director Lisa James.

Kalavari Samsaram (1982) is an Indian Telugu-language drama film directed by K. S. Rami Reddy, starring Krishna and Sridevi. Based on the novel *Agni Pariksha*, it follows a man who takes charge of his debt-ridden joint family after his father's death. The music was composed by K. V. Mahadevan.

The Czech units of measurement were historical systems used in Bohemia, Moravia, Silesia, and Prague, varying by region. Common units included the *stopa* (foot), *loket* (ell), and *korec* (dry measure), with lengths and capacities differing across localities. These were later replaced by the metric system.

The National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), established in 1975, is a "nonpartisan public officials’ association composed of sitting state legislators" from the states, territories and commonwealths of the United States.

John Paton (died 9 May 1684) was a Scottish Covenanter and military officer. A veteran of battles including Marston Moor and Bothwell Bridge, he was executed in Edinburgh for his role in the Covenanter uprisings.

Steven Williams (born January 7, 1949) is an American actor known for his roles in television and film. After serving in the U.S. Army, he began his acting career in *Cooley High* (1975) and gained recognition for roles in *The Blues Brothers* (1980) and *21 Jump Street* (1987–1991) as Captain Adam Fuller. He later played recurring characters in *The X-Files* (as X) and *Supernatural* (as Rufus Turner). His recent work includes *The Chi* (2018–present) and *Locke & Key* (2020). Williams has also appeared in numerous guest roles across TV series and films. He is married to talent agent Ann Geddes and has two daughters.

Acacia parramattensis, commonly known as Parramatta wattle or Sydney green wattle, is a species of flowering tree in the family Fabaceae, native to central New South Wales, Australia. Growing 2–15 meters tall, it has bipinnate leaves and produces spherical yellow flower heads from late spring to early summer. First described by botanist Mary Tindale in 1962, it thrives in dry sclerophyll forests, often on shale or alluvial soils. The species plays a role in local ecosystems, supporting insects and birds, and is culturally significant in Dharawal Aboriginal mythology. Though not widely cultivated, it is valued for fast growth and adaptability in revegetation projects.

Gene Winstead (born 1951) is an American politician and businessman from Minnesota. Winstead was first elected Mayor of Bloomington, Minnesota in 2000. He had previously served as a city council member for 5 years. His 20 year tenure as mayor is the longest in Bloomington history.

Soyuz TMA-11 was a 2007 Russian crewed spaceflight to the International Space Station (ISS). Launched on October 10, 2007, from Baikonur Cosmodrome, it carried Expedition 16 crew members Yuri Malenchenko and Peggy Whitson, along with Malaysian spaceflight participant Sheikh Muszaphar Shukor. The spacecraft docked with the ISS two days later. After 191 days in orbit, it returned to Earth on April 19, 2008, carrying Malenchenko, Whitson, and South Korean astronaut Yi So-yeon. The landing was marked by a ballistic reentry, a steeper-than-normal descent caused by a malfunction, resulting in an off-target landing. An investigation later attributed the anomaly to a failed pyro-bolt, possibly due to prolonged exposure to the ISS's electrical environment.

Soyuz TMA-11 was a human spaceflight mission using a Soyuz-TMA spacecraft to transport personnel to and from the International Space Station (ISS). The mission began at 13:22 UTC on October 10, 2007, when the spacecraft was launched from the Baikonur Cosmodrome by a Soyuz FG launch vehicle. It brought to the station two members of the ISS Expedition 16 crew, as well as Sheikh Muszaphar, the first Malaysian in space. TMA-11 remained at the station as an escape craft, and returned safely to Earth on April 19, 2008, after it had been replaced by Soyuz TMA-12. Although the vehicle landed safely, it suffered a partial separation failure which caused a ballistic re-entry that in turn caused it to land 475 km from the intended landing point.
Patrick Pothuizen (born 15 May 1972) is a Dutch football manager and former player who is the head coach of Eerste Klasse club Achilles '29.

Pa amb tomàquet (IPA-ca) ("Bread with tomato"), is a traditional food of Catalan, Aragonese and Balearic cuisine. Pa amb tomàquet is considered a staple of Catalan cuisine and identity. While considered a signature toast dish in the Catalan Countries, it is common in bars throughout the rest of Spain, where it is also known as pan tumaca.

It consists of bread, which may or may not be toasted, with tomato rubbed over and seasoned with olive oil and salt.

It is considered one of the typical examples that define the Mediterranean diet, extended as a traditional recipe throughout the Catalan Countries.

It is popularly consumed on its own as a snack or a tapa with any meal, from breakfast to dinner.

Christian College, Geelong is an independent, co-educational Christian school in Highton, Victoria, Australia. Founded in 1980 with 40 students, it has grown to over 1,900 students across multiple campuses. The Junior School occupies the former Glastonbury Orphanage, while the Middle School is housed in the historic St. Augustine's Orphanage building. The Senior Campus, acquired in 1999, was previously part of Geelong Grammar School. A fourth campus, the Bellarine Campus, opened in 1996 in repurposed council offices.  

The school operates a house system with four houses—Penman, Flynn, Taylor, and Burrows—each named after notable Christian figures. Its music program includes orchestras, bands, and choirs, which have earned awards at competitions such as the Melbourne School Bands Festival and the Pacific Basin Music Festival. The school also stages biennial musical productions.  

In 2015, Christian College gained media attention when three students arrived at a Year 12 farewell event in an unauthorized helicopter, leading to the cancellation of the following year's celebrations.

The Gator Guard Drill Team, abbreviated GGDT, is a precision drill team and military fraternity based at the University of Florida. The team is named after the Florida Gators, the mascot of the University of Florida. Founded in 1953, the Gator Guard is most widely known for its annual performances in the New Orleans Mardi Gras Parades. In 2016, the team consisted of 31 active male and female members.

Difunta Correa is a figure in Argentine folk Catholicism, venerated as a popular saint despite lacking official recognition by the Catholic Church. According to legend, she died in the 1840s while crossing the San Juan desert in search of her sick husband, but her infant child miraculously survived by nursing from her lifeless body. Her shrine in Vallecito, San Juan, has grown into a major pilgrimage site, featuring numerous chapels filled with votive offerings. She is especially revered by cattle herders, truck drivers, and gauchos, who leave water bottles at roadside shrines to "quench her eternal thirst." Her feast days coincide with Easter and All Souls' Day (November 2), drawing large crowds.

Mystic Comics was a Golden Age comic book series published by Timely Comics (later Marvel Comics) in two volumes. The first (1940–1942) introduced characters like the Destroyer, Blazing Skull, and the original Black Widow. The second (1944–1945) featured the Angel and the Young Allies. A separate Mystic horror anthology (1951–1957) was later published by Atlas Comics.

The Koza riot was a violent uprising on December 20, 1970, in Koza, Okinawa (now Okinawa City), sparked by tensions between Okinawan civilians and U.S. military personnel. The unrest began after a drunk American serviceman struck an Okinawan pedestrian, leading to clashes with taxi drivers and a growing crowd. The riot escalated into widespread destruction, with rioters burning over 70 cars, attacking servicemen, and breaching Kadena Air Base. At least 87 people were injured, and 82 were arrested. The incident reflected deep resentment over U.S. military presence and legal immunity in Okinawa.

In the theory of von Neumann algebras, a part of the mathematical field of functional analysis, Tomita–Takesaki theory is a method for constructing modular automorphisms of von Neumann algebras from the polar decomposition of a certain involution. It is essential for the theory of type III factors, and has led to a good structure theory for these previously intractable objects.

The theory was introduced by Minoru Tomita (1967), but his work was hard to follow and mostly unpublished, and little notice was taken of it until Masamichi Takesaki (1970) wrote an account of Tomita's theory.
Alimi Ballard (born October 17, 1977) is an American television actor. He is best known for his role as FBI agent David Sinclair on the CBS police procedural Numb3rs (2005–2010).

*The Highwomen* is the debut studio album by the American country supergroup the Highwomen, released on September 6, 2019. Produced by Dave Cobb, the album features collaborations with artists like Sheryl Crow and Yola. It received critical acclaim and debuted at number 10 on the *Billboard* 200.

Lyme, New Hampshire, is a rural town in Grafton County, incorporated in 1761. Located along the Connecticut River, it features historic landmarks such as the Lyme Common, the Lyme Inn (1809), and a row of preserved 19th-century horse sheds. The town includes the villages of Lyme and Lyme Center and is home to scenic landscapes, including Smarts Mountain and parts of the Appalachian Trail. As of 2020, Lyme had a population of 1,745.
F.C. Netanya (Lang-he) is an Israeli football club based in Netanya. The club currently plays in Liga Gimel Sharon division.

Faerie Tale Theatre (also known as *Shelley Duvall's Faerie Tale Theatre*) is an American fantasy anthology television series that aired on Showtime from 1982 to 1987. Created and hosted by Shelley Duvall, the show presented live-action adaptations of classic fairy tales, featuring an ensemble of Hollywood actors and directors, including Robin Williams, Francis Ford Coppola, and Tim Burton. Each episode opened with Duvall introducing the story, followed by a whimsical retelling with elaborate costumes and stylized sets. The series was one of the earliest examples of original cable programming and received critical acclaim, winning a Peabody Award, a TCA Award, and a Golden CableACE Award. It was later syndicated on networks like PBS and the Disney Channel. Duvall produced three follow-up anthologies: *Tall Tales & Legends*, *Nightmare Classics*, and *Bedtime Stories*.

Oak Grove is a home rule-class city adjacent to the Fort Campbell army base in Christian County, Kentucky, in the United States. The population was 7,489 as of the 2010 U.S. Census. It is part of the Clarksville, Tennessee metropolitan area.

New Hampshire state elections in 2018 were held on Tuesday, November 6, 2018, with the primary elections being held on June 5, 2018. Voters elected 2 members to the United States House of Representatives, the Governor of New Hampshire, all five members to the Executive Council, all 24 members to the New Hampshire Senate, and all 400 members to the New Hampshire House of Representatives, among other local elected offices.
Domingos Sousa Coutinho Meneses Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a central defender for La Liga club Getafe and the Portugal national team.

Speshow is the fourth studio album by Taiwanese Mandopop singer Show Lo, released on 17 November 2006 by Avex Taiwan. A dance-pop record, it debuted at number one on Taiwan's G-Music Mandarin, Combo, and Five Music charts, remaining on the charts for up to 27 weeks. The singles "Good Friends" (好朋友) and "Dance Gate" (精舞門) were both nominated for Songs of the Year at the 2007 Metro Radio Mandarin Music Awards, with the latter also winning Top 10 Song of the Year at the 2007 HITO Radio Music Awards. The album was released in four editions, excluding pre-order versions.

Petr Rada (born 21 August 1958) is a Czech football coach and former player. He represented Czechoslovakia internationally in the 1980s and managed the Czech Republic national football team between 2008 and 2009. He is well known for his arguments with fans or trainer colleagues and also for impulsive style of coaching.

Before being named national team manager in July 2008, Rada worked as an assistant for then national team coach Karel Brückner from 2006 to 2008, and also as an assistant to Jozef Chovanec from 1998 to 2001 when Chovanec was manager of the Czech national team. Rada has also been head manager of eight various Czech clubs: FK Teplice (on four separate occasions), FC Viktoria Plzeň, FK Jablonec, FC Slovan Liberec, SK Slavia Prague, FC Vysočina Jihlava, Příbram and Sparta Prague.

Pheona Nabasa Gladys Wall, commonly known as Pheona Wall, is a Ugandan lawyer, Public Relation Officer and serves as the president of Uganda Law Society since September 2020. She served as a lecturer of Law at Kampala International University.

The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is the central bank of South Africa, established on 30 June 1921. Headquartered in Pretoria, it regulates monetary policy, issues the South African rand (ZAR), and maintains financial stability. Unlike most central banks, the SARB is privately owned, with shares held by around 700 shareholders, though dividends are capped at 10 cents per share annually. The bank is governed by a board of directors, including a governor (currently Lesetja Kganyago) and three deputy governors, appointed by the President of South Africa in consultation with the Minister of Finance.  

The SARB succeeded the Bank of England in managing South Africa’s monetary system. It holds foreign reserves of approximately $38.9 billion (as of available data) and sets key interest rates, such as the repo rate (3.75%). The South African government has proposed nationalizing the bank, though this has not yet been implemented. The SARB operates under multiple official names in South Africa’s 11 official languages.

The architecture of Poland reflects the nation's historical and cultural evolution, spanning styles from Pre-Romanesque to contemporary designs. Key periods include Gothic (Wawel Cathedral, Malbork Castle), Renaissance (Wawel Castle, Zamość Old Town), Baroque (Wilanów Palace), and Neoclassical (Palace on the Isle). The 19th century saw eclecticism and Art Nouveau, while the interwar period embraced modernism and functionalism. Post-1945 architecture includes socialist realism (Palace of Culture and Science) and brutalism, followed by postmodern and neo-modern trends after 1989. Notable contemporary works include the Szczecin Philharmonic and Museum of the History of Polish Jews.

Millennium Fantasy for Piano and Orchestra is a piano concerto written by the American composer Ellen Taaffe Zwilich. The work was commissioned by the pianist Jeffrey Biegel in association with the Adele Marcus Foundation, the Abraham J. & Phyllis Katz Foundation, the South Florida Council of the Chopin Foundation of the United States, Isa and Marvin Leibowitz, the American Music Center, and a consortium of 27 American orchestras. It was first performed by Jeffrey Biegel and the Cincinnati Symphony Orchestra under the direction of Jesús López Cobos in Cincinnati on September 22, 2000.
Sergio Ferrer Marrero (born January 29, 1951) is a former Major League Baseball shortstop.

Frederick William Twort FRS (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist and was the original discoverer in 1915 of bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria). He studied medicine at St Thomas's Hospital, London, was superintendent of the Brown Institute for Animals (a pathology research centre), and was a professor of bacteriology at the University of London. He researched into Johne's disease, a chronic intestinal infection of cattle, and also discovered that vitamin K is needed by growing leprosy bacteria.

Sanan Suleymanov is an Azerbaijani Greco-Roman wrestler. He won the silver medal in the 77 kg event at the 2021 World Wrestling Championships held in Oslo, Norway. In 2020, he won the gold medal in the 77 kg event at the European Wrestling Championships held in Rome, Italy.

Kamaha Castle (鎌刃城, Kamaha jō) was a Sengoku period yamashiro-style Japanese castle located in the Banba neighborhood of the city of Maibara, Shiga Prefecture, in the Kansai region of Japan. Its ruins have been protected as a National Historic Site since 2005.
Nicholas Nichols Cox (January 6, 1837 – May 2, 1912) was an American politician and a member of the United States House of Representatives for the Tennessee's 7th congressional district.

The Asian Qualification Tournament for the 2020 Men's Olympic Volleyball Tournament was a volleyball tournament for men's national teams held in Jiangmen, China from 7 to 12 January 2020. 8 teams played in the tournament and the winners Iran qualified for the 2020 Summer Olympics (postponed to 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic).

Big Thunder Ski Jumping Centre was a twin ski jumping hill located in Thunder Bay in Northwestern Ontario, Canada. It constitutes part of Big Thunder National Training Centre. The first hills were built by Knute and Thor Hansen and opened in 1963. They were originally known as Lille Norway Ski Area, then Mt. Norway Ski Area, and Sundance Northwest Resort before taking the current name. The large and normal hills were built in 1974 and the venue was taken over by the provincial government in 1985. The hills hosted 29 FIS Ski Jumping World Cup and 50 Canadian Ski Jumping Championships tournaments between 1975 and 1995, climaxing with the FIS Nordic World Ski Championships 1995. Funding was then cut and the venue has since been closed and unmaintained.

Akhtarul Wasey (born September 1, 1951) is the president of Maulana Azad University, Jodhpur, India, and a former professor of Islamic Studies. He taught at Jamia Millia Islamia (Central University) in New Delhi, where he remains professor emeritus in the Department of Islamic Studies.

The PKP class ET22 is a Polish electric freight locomotive, produced from 1969 to 1989. With 1,183 units built, it became the most numerous standard-gauge electric locomotive in Europe. Based on the EU07 design, it features a Co-Co wheel arrangement and a maximum power of 3,000 kW (4,000 hp). Modified versions were also exported to Morocco.
Joseph Buck (born December 10, 2002) is an American soccer player who plays as a midfielder for Georgetown Hoyas.
John Paton was a Scottish soldier and Covenanter. He was executed at the Grassmarket on 9 May 1684 largely for his actions at the Battle of Bothwell Bridge.

The men's Asian qualification tournament for volleyball at the 2020 Summer Olympics was held in Jiangmen, China, from 7 to 12 January 2020. Eight teams competed, with Iran winning the tournament and securing an Olympic berth, while China finished as runners-up. The event served as the final continental qualifier for Asia.

Egon Albrecht-Lemke (19 May 1918 – 25 August 1944) was a German Luftwaffe fighter pilot during World War II. Credited with 25 aerial victories, including six four-engine bombers, he commanded fighter units on both the Eastern and Western Fronts. He was awarded the Knight's Cross of the Iron Cross before being killed in action by USAAF fighters in 1944.

Pierre Galet (1921 – 31 December 2019) was a French ampelographer and viticulturist, best known for his extensive work in identifying and classifying grape varieties. After studying viticulture at the University of Montpellier during World War II, he became a professor there from 1946 to 1989, mentoring prominent ampelographers. Galet traveled globally to resolve disputes over grape varieties and opposed bans on certain vines in Europe. He identified over 100 Pinot family varieties and exposed mislabeled vines, such as California’s "Pinot blanc" actually being Melon de Bourgogne. His publications, including *Cépages et vignoble de France* and *Dictionnaire encyclopédique des cépages*, became foundational works in viticulture. He received honors such as the *Ordre du Mérite Agricole* and recognition from the OIV.

Alfred Webb (1834–1908) was an Irish Quaker, writer, and politician. A member of the Liberal Party, he served as an MP for West Waterford (1890–1895) and later for Finsbury Central (1892–1895). He was a supporter of Irish Home Rule, land reform, and anti-imperialist causes. Webb also presided over the Indian National Congress in 1894 and promoted anti-racism through his involvement with *Anti-Caste* journal. His family's printing press in Dublin supported progressive movements, including abolitionism and women's rights.

"Heartache Tonight" is a song by the Eagles from their 1979 album *The Long Run*. Co-written by Don Henley, Glenn Frey, Bob Seger, and J. D. Souther, it blends hard rock and blues rock with energetic handclaps and vocal harmonies. Released as a single, it won a Grammy for Best Rock Performance by a Duo or Group with Vocal. The song was later covered by Conway Twitty, reaching No. 6 on the *Billboard* Hot Country Singles chart in 1983. Other versions include renditions by John Anderson, Michael Bublé, and Tom Jones.

Kate Walsh (née Shimmin; born in 1992) is an Australian netball player in the Suncorp Super Netball league, playing for the Sunshine Coast Lightning. In August 2019, Jess Thirlby the new England Roses Coach announced Shimmin would join the full time England Roses programme.

Shimmin grew up in South Australia and was a part of the South Australian Institute of Sport Volleyball program before being offered a SASI Netball scholarship which she chose over volleyball. When she finished High School she moved to Canberra where she developed her skills at the Australian Institute of Sport.

The Murakumo-class destroyers (叢雲型駆逐艦, Murakumo-gata kuchikukan) ("Gathering Clouds") were a class of six torpedo boat destroyers (TBDs) of the Imperial Japanese Navy, built in Britain in 1897–99. The class is also sometimes referred to as the Shinonome-class destroyers (東雲駆逐艦, Shinonome-gata kuchikukan)("Daybreak"). All were named after celestial phenomena.
